title: French green clay cold-process soap
subtitle: A detoxifying bar coloured and textured by French green clay, on an olive, coconut, and castor base
excerpt: French green clay gives this cold-process bar a muted sage-green colour and a soft, smooth texture. The clay is a natural absorbent mineral that draws oil and impurities from the skin's surface. Combined with a conditioning olive-dominant oil base and a small castor addition for lather, the result is a mild, creamy bar suited to normal and oily skin.
sourceNotes: SAP values used: olive oil 0.134, coconut oil 0.190, castor oil 0.128. 5% superfat; 33% lye discount. Reference: Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997), oil and additive chapters. French green clay (illite) as a soap additive: Bramble Berry cosmetic clays guide (2023). Clay is dispersed in a small portion of oil before addition to avoid clumping. Per-batch SAP calculation via SoapCalc.

recipe.batchNotes: Recalculate lye weight for any change to oil amounts. Clay does not affect the lye calculation; it is an additive only.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Allow 2 days in the mould before unmoulding. Minimum 4-week cure; 6 weeks gives a harder, milder bar.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Fully cured bars keep two years in a cool, dark place on an open rack. Discard any bar showing orange oily spots (DOS) or a sharp paint-like smell.

glossaryTerms.definition: The point at which the soap batter thickens enough that a drizzle on the surface holds its shape briefly. The moment for adding clays and other late-stage additives.
The percentage of oil left unsaponified in the finished bar. At 5%, the bar is conditioning and mild without being soft.
The reaction between fat and lye that produces soap and glycerine. The lye weight in this recipe is calculated for the specific oil blend and 5% superfat.

projectSchedule.title: Unmould and cut
projectSchedule.body: Turn out after 2 days. Cut into bars. Wear protective gloves; the bars are caustic until fully cured.

projectSchedule.title: Four-week check
projectSchedule.body: Test a sliver on the inside of the forearm. Ready to use from this point. The clay colour will have softened to a pale sage green.

projectSchedule.title: Cure complete
projectSchedule.body: Fully cured bar. Hard, mild, and stable for two years.

content.text: French green clay (illite) colours this bar a soft sage green and gives it a smooth, fine texture when used on skin. Clay is a natural absorbent mineral. Dispersed through the soap batter at
trace
, it distributes evenly through the bar. The oil base runs 60% olive, 35% coconut, and 5% castor, giving good conditioning and lather. At 5%
superfat
and 33% lye discount, this is a mild bar suited to normal and oily skin types. Yield is 8 bars of around 120 g each.
Put on protective gloves and goggles before starting. Lye handling comes first.
Ingredients

items.prepNote: 35% of oil weight; melt if solid
NaOH at 99% purity; calculated for 5% superfat
Cosmetic grade; dispersed in 2 tablespoons of the weighed olive oil before use

content.text: Method
Disperse the clay

Measure 2 tablespoons of the weighed olive oil into a small bowl. Add the French green clay and stir until there are no lumps. Set aside. Dispersing clay in oil before adding it to the batter prevents clumps in the finished bar.
Mix the lye solution

Work in a well-ventilated space with goggles and protective gloves on. Weigh the sodium hydroxide separately. Pour the distilled water into the HDPE pitcher. Add the lye to the water in a steady stream, stirring with a stainless-steel spoon. Never add water to lye. The solution heats sharply; stand back. Allow to cool to 38 to 42°C.
Combine and blend to trace

Melt the coconut oil if solid and combine all oils in a heat-safe bowl. Cool to 38 to 42°C. Pour the lye solution into the oils in a steady stream. Stick-blend in short bursts until light trace. Stir in the clay-oil mixture quickly and blend for 5 seconds. Pour into the mould.
Insulate, unmould, and cure
Wrap the mould in a towel. After 2 days the
saponification
reaction will be complete. Unmould, cut into bars wearing protective gloves, and arrange on a rack with space between each. Cure for 4 to 6 weeks in a cool, dry room.
Storage and shelf life
Two years in a cool, dark place on an open rack. Avoid sealed containers. Discard on signs of DOS (orange spots) or a paint-like smell.
